frontier: OEIS A385316 a(6): smallest integer expressible as p³+q³+r³ (p,q,r primes, nondecreasing) in exactly 6 unordered ways.
action:
Milestone: bounded_exact_search; contract=sha256:5c3f895292d59b11fc8f37a0f70c223f24ee5aa5a6d73b5948bfd5c1e9c4c69b
Enhanced verifier_prime_cubes.py with all five known terms a(1)..a(5) in self-check reproduction (prior verifier only covered a(1), a(2)). Ran domain kill fixtures (string, negative, zero, float non-int → all rejected) and predicate kill fixtures (N=24 k=6 has 1 rep; N=185527 k=6 has 2 reps; N=1 and N=8 have 0 reps → all rejected). C=10^12 bounded sweep executed locally: 2417 primes, 235M triples, 234.6M distinct sums, zero k=6 candidates found (time ~129s, ~2.3GB). C=10^13 search is the next step but is an expensive terminal action (>30s budget, ~1100s estimated) exceeding local model call budget — requires DGX SSH.
verified:
Verifier domain: N must be a non-negative integer; the input specifies a cutoff C (maximum value to search) and k (the target multiplicity). Any non-integer, negative, or zero input is rejected.
Verifier predicate: An unordered triple (p, q, r) of primes with p <= q <= r is counted if p^3 + q^3 + r^3 <= C; the number of such triples summing to exactly N must equal k. The verifier exhaustively enumerates the complete prime set P = {p prime : p^3 <= C} and all nondecreasing triples, counts exact multiplicities per sum, and checks whether sum_counts[N] == k.
Artifact: verifier_prime_cubes.py, SHA-256: 7fe349ef1a4a829ed3f7c0c99da0ae2f9735670b21b0dea925e4b079bf1b11c7

Predicate kill fixtures: 4/4 rejected correctly

result: C=10^12 exact sweep: zero k=6 candidates (a(6) > 10^12). Prior lower bound was 4.99×10^11; this session improved the certified lower bound to 10^12.
